variables.ω_d: Angular frequency of the external driving force (rad/s)
variables.ω₀: Natural angular frequency of the oscillating system = √(k/m) (rad/s)
variables.F₀: Amplitude of the external periodic driving force (N)
variables.A_steady: Steady-state amplitude of the driven oscillation (m)
variables.Q: Quality factor = ω₀/(2γ) = mω₀/b — measure of sharpness of resonance

locked_facts: Steady-state amplitude A = F₀ / √{m²(ω₀² − ω_d²)² + b²ω_d²} — maximum near ω_d = ω₀
Resonance occurs when the driving frequency ω_d equals (or is very close to) the natural frequency ω₀ — amplitude reaches maximum
At resonance, the system absorbs maximum energy from the driving force per cycle
The sharpness of resonance is characterised by the quality factor Q = mω₀/b — higher Q means sharper resonance peak and lower damping
With zero damping (b = 0), amplitude at resonance would theoretically become infinite — in reality, damping always limits it
The driven oscillation eventually settles to the driving frequency ω_d, regardless of the system's natural frequency ω₀
truth_statements: In forced oscillations, an external periodic force drives the system and the steady-state oscillation occurs at the driving frequency, not the natural frequency
Resonance is the condition where the amplitude of forced oscillation becomes maximum, occurring when the driving frequency matches the natural frequency
The amplitude at resonance is inversely proportional to the damping — smaller damping means larger resonance amplitude
The quality factor Q determines how sharp the resonance peak is — high Q systems resonate strongly at a narrow frequency range
Resonance is responsible for many physical phenomena: tuning a radio, shattering a glass with sound, soldiers breaking step on bridges

aha_moment.what_to_show: An amplitude vs. driving frequency graph with multiple curves for different damping values. As damping decreases, the resonance peak grows taller and sharper. At very low damping, the peak is extremely tall and narrow at ω_d = ω₀. A vertical dashed line marks ω₀. The driven system's amplitude visibly explodes as ω_d approaches ω₀.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: The resonance peak towers above the rest of the curve at ω_d = ω₀. Lower damping makes the peak dramatically taller — the system is 'tuned' to its natural frequency. Away from ω₀, the amplitude drops sharply.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think any frequency should produce the same response — this shows the system is exquisitely selective, responding overwhelmingly at its natural frequency and weakly at all others

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Left panel: a spring-mass system with a damper, attached to an external oscillating driver (e.g., a motor-driven arm). The driving frequency ω_d and the system's natural frequency ω₀ are labelled. Right panel: empty amplitude vs. frequency axes ready for the resonance curve.
simulation_states.emphasis: Two frequencies at play — the natural frequency ω₀ of the free system and the driving frequency ω_d of the external force. They can be different.

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Left panel: the system oscillates at a fixed ω_d far from ω₀ — steady-state amplitude is small. Right panel: a dot appears on the resonance curve at the current (ω_d, A) point. The driver frequency slowly sweeps from low to high, and amplitude changes — small far from ω₀, rising sharply near ω₀.
simulation_states.emphasis: As the driving frequency approaches ω₀, the amplitude grows dramatically. This frequency-selective response is the essence of resonance.

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Right panel: three resonance curves (A vs. ω_d) superimposed for low, medium, and high damping. The low-damping curve has a towering sharp peak at ω₀. High-damping curve has a broad, low hump. Left panel: the system at resonance (ω_d = ω₀) with low damping shows dramatically large oscillations.
simulation_states.emphasis: Resonance is a frequency match — the system responds maximally when driven at its natural frequency. Less damping → taller, sharper peak. This selectivity is why a specific note can shatter a specific glass.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust driving frequency ω_d with one slider and damping b with another. Left panel shows the spring-mass system oscillating in real time at the chosen ω_d. Right panel shows the full resonance curve with a moving dot at the current operating point. Amplitude and Q factor update live.
simulation_states.emphasis: Sweep ω_d through ω₀ and watch the amplitude peak. Then increase damping — the peak broadens and flattens. Decrease damping — the peak sharpens. Q factor = ω₀m/b displayed.

common_misconceptions.belief: Resonance means the system oscillates at infinite amplitude
trigger_phrases: resonance mein system toot jaata hai
amplitude infinity ho jaata hai resonance pe
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT Ch. 13 explains that in any real system, damping is always present. The amplitude at resonance is A = F₀/(bω₀) — finite and inversely proportional to damping. Only in the idealised case of zero damping (b = 0) does the formula give infinite amplitude, which is physically unrealisable.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show resonance curves with different damping — even at the smallest damping, the peak is tall but finite. A label shows A_max = F₀/(bω₀).

common_misconceptions.belief: The system always oscillates at its natural frequency, regardless of the driving frequency
trigger_phrases: system apni natural frequency pe hi chalega
driving frequency se koi fark nahi padta
natural frequency pe hi oscillate karega hamesha
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT states that in the steady state, the system oscillates at the DRIVING frequency ω_d, not its natural frequency ω₀. The natural frequency determines at which driving frequency the amplitude is maximum (resonance), but the system follows the driver's frequency. Only in free oscillations does the system oscillate at ω₀.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Set ω_d far from ω₀ — the system visibly oscillates at ω_d (count cycles against a clock). The system follows the driver, not its own preference.

legend.meaning: Resonance curve: amplitude A vs. driving frequency ω_d
Natural frequency ω₀ — resonance occurs here
Low damping — high Q, strong frequency selectivity
High damping — low Q, weak selectivity
